title: Ten Minutes Outside, Daily
subtitle: A one-week activity for building a daily connection to the outside world
excerpt: A one-week activity: ten minutes outside each day, without a phone. A simple practice for reconnecting with the natural world when it has receded from daily life.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The benefits of time in natural settings are well-documented in public-domain environmental psychology literature (Rachel and Stephen Kaplan's Attention Restoration Theory, 1989).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you have been indoors or screen-occupied for too many days in a row and the outside world has started to feel irrelevant.

content.text: Ten minutes outside, without a phone, every day for a week. The activity is as small as it sounds. The effect, for people who have been mostly indoors, is not.

The activity is ten minutes outside each day, with no particular destination and no phone in your hand. It counts if you stand in the garden. It counts if you walk to the end of the road and back. It counts if you sit on the front step. The requirement is outside and without a screen.

The phone splits the attention between the screen and the environment. The purpose of the ten minutes is to let the attention rest on the environment: what you can see, what you can hear, what the temperature is, what is moving. This is closer to a restorative practice than an exercise one. The attention needs to be available for the environment for the practice to do anything.
Pick a time that is consistent: after the school run, after lunch, before the evening starts. Consistency matters more than the time of day.
Leave the phone inside, or put it in your pocket on silent. It does not need to come out.
Go outside. Walk or stand. No goal.
Come back in after ten minutes. Notice whether the first few minutes after returning feel different from the first few minutes of a day you haven't gone outside.

Many people who do this consistently for a week report that the urge to pick up the phone as a default occupant of downtime reduces slightly. The outside time gives the attention somewhere else to land.

Original to homemade.education. The restorative effects of time in natural settings are documented in environmental psychology, particularly Rachel and Stephen Kaplan's Attention Restoration Theory (1989), which describes how natural environments restore directed attention capacity.
